is a [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi"), of the [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") confederation of New Zealand, tracing its descent from [Whakaue Kaipapa](/wiki/Whakaue_Kaipapa "Whakaue Kaipapa"), son of Uenuku-kopakō, and grandson of [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi"). The tribe lives in the [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") district and descends from the [Arawa](/wiki/Arawa_(canoe) "Arawa (canoe)") waka.[[1]](#cite_note-tkmentry-1)[[2]](#cite_note-taentry-2) The Ngāti Whakaue village [Ōhinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") is within the township of Rotorua. The Ngāti Whakaue chief Pūkākī is depicted on the [New Zealand 20 cent coin](/wiki/New_Zealand_20_cent_coin "New Zealand 20 cent coin").[[3]](#cite_note-twodollarcoin-3)

["Te Puni Kōkiri iwi profile"](https://web.archive.org/web/20180529203402/http://www.tkm.govt.nz/iwi/te-ure-o-uenukukopako-ngati-whakaue/). *tkm.govt.nz*. [Te Puni Kōkiri](/wiki/Te_Puni_K%C5%8Dkiri "Te Puni Kōkiri"), [New Zealand Government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government"). Archived from [the original](http://www.tkm.govt.nz/iwi/te-ure-o-uenukukopako-ngati-whakaue/) on 29 May 2018. Retrieved 29 May 2018.

Tapsell, Paul. ["Te Ara iwi profile"](https://teara.govt.nz/en/te-arawa). *[Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand](/wiki/Te_Ara:_The_Encyclopedia_of_New_Zealand "Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand")*. Retrieved 29 May 2017.
["Two Dollar Coin"](http://www.rbnz.govt.nz/notes_and_coins/coins/0160568.html). *rbnz.govt.nz*. Reserve Bank of New Zealand. Retrieved 29 May 2018.
[*Ngāti Whakaue Education Endowment Trust Board*](https://vps.ngatiwhakaue-eetb.org.nz/index.php#AllGrants). Official website.
[*The Fenton Agreement: The setting up of Rotorua*](https://www.gtas.nz/the-fenton-agreement). *[Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") Stories Digital Storehouse.*

[*Story: Te Kōti Whenua – Māori Land Court*](https://teara.govt.nz/en/photograph/36137/judge-fenton-1890). *[Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand](/wiki/Te_Ara:_The_Encyclopedia_of_New_Zealand "Te Ara: The Encyclopedia of New Zealand")*
["Our School History"](https://www.rbhs.school.nz/our-school/school-identity/our-history/). Rotorua Boys' High School.
Kevin J. Lyall - School Historian. [*Rotorua Boys' High School - An Historical Introduction*](https://www.rbhs.school.nz/downloads/Rotorua-Boys-High-School---An-Historical-Introduction-1.pdf). p.26.
Ngāti Whakaue Education Trust Board: [*Annual Report 2022-23*](https://ngatiwhakaue.iwi.nz/wp-content/uploads/2023/11/Ngati-Whakaue-Tribal-Lands_Annual-Report-2023_email-web.pdf).

["About Te Arawa"](https://web.archive.org/web/20150414020602/http://tearawaonline.com/about/). *Te Arawa Online*. Te Arawa Communications. Archived from [the original](http://tearawaonline.com/about/) on 14 April 2015. Retrieved 27 April 2015.
"Major transformation for Te Arawa iwi radio station". No. 14. Kia Hiwa Ra. September 1993. p. 8.

["Rotorua"](https://web.archive.org/web/20090415173756/http://www.theradiovault.net/rotorua.htm). *Welcome to the Radio Vault*. New Zealand: The Radio Vault. 18 January 2009. Archived from [the original](http://www.theradiovault.net/rotorua.htm) on 15 April 2009. Retrieved 12 June 2015.
["Iwi Radio Coverage"](http://www.maorimedia.co.nz/Iwi_Radio_Coverage.pdf) (PDF). *maorimedia.co.nz*. Māori Media Network. 2007. Retrieved 14 June 2015.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Māori iwi (tribe) in Aotearoa (New Zealand)

Māori iwi in New Zealand

| Ngāti Whakaue | |
| --- | --- |
| [Iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") (tribe) in [Māoridom](/wiki/M%C4%81oridom "Māoridom") | |
| Rohe (region) | [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") |
| Waka (canoe) | [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") |

**Ngāti Whakaue** is a [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people") [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi"), of the [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") confederation of New Zealand, tracing its descent from [Whakaue Kaipapa](/wiki/Whakaue_Kaipapa "Whakaue Kaipapa"), son of Uenuku-kopakō, and grandson of [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi"). The tribe lives in the [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") district and descends from the [Arawa](/wiki/Arawa_(canoe) "Arawa (canoe)") waka.[[1]](#cite_note-tkmentry-1)[[2]](#cite_note-taentry-2) The Ngāti Whakaue village [Ōhinemutu](/wiki/Ohinemutu "Ohinemutu") is within the township of Rotorua. The Ngāti Whakaue chief Pūkākī is depicted on the [New Zealand 20 cent coin](/wiki/New_Zealand_20_cent_coin "New Zealand 20 cent coin").[[3]](#cite_note-twodollarcoin-3)

The Ngāti Whakaue Education Trust Board administers grants to a range of education projects,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and has been a source of funding for [Rotorua Boys' High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Boys%27_High_School "Rotorua Boys' High School"), [Rotorua Girls' High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Girls%27_High_School "Rotorua Girls' High School"), [Rotorua Lakes High School](/wiki/Rotorua_Lakes_High_School "Rotorua Lakes High School") and [Western Heights High School](/wiki/Western_Heights_High_School "Western Heights High School") since its establishment in 1881 under the [Fenton](/wiki/Francis_Dart_Fenton "Francis Dart Fenton") Agreement.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) Revenues to the Trust derive primarily from commercial leases in the [Rotorua CBD](/wiki/Rotorua_Central "Rotorua Central"), which increased sharply upon the expiration of 99-year leases in 1980. In 2023, the Trust reported a net profit after tax of $9,004,155.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[Te Arawa FM](/wiki/Te_Arawa_FM "Te Arawa FM") is the radio station of [Te Arawa](/wiki/Te_Arawa "Te Arawa") iwi, including Ngāti Whakaue, [Ngāti Pikiao](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Pikiao "Ngāti Pikiao") and [Tūhourangi](/wiki/T%C5%ABhourangi "Tūhourangi"). It was established in the early 1980s and became a charitable entity in November 1990.[[10]](#cite_note-tearawa-10) The station underwent a major transformation in 1993, becoming Whanau FM.[[11]](#cite_note-transformarawa-11) One of the station's frequencies was taken over by [Mai FM](/wiki/Mai_FM "Mai FM") in 1998; the other became Pumanawa FM before later reverting to Te Arawa FM.[[12]](#cite_note-rotorua-12) It is available on 89.0 FM in [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua").[[13]](#cite_note-maorimedia-13)
